description: A class I antiarrhythmic (sodium-channel blocker) once widely used for ventricular and atrial arrhythmias. The liver acetylates it to an active metabolite, NAPA, at a rate set by an inherited 'acetylator' trait — a textbook example of pharmacogenetic variability.

halfLife.conditions: Procainamide elimination half-life 2.4 ± 0.7 h in FAST acetylators after a single 750 mg oral dose in healthy subjects (Wierzchowiecki 1980) — the illustrative phenotype modelled here (see notes). The range [1.7, 3.1] is that 2.4 ± 0.7 h FAST-acetylator variability, and it is deliberately kept WITHIN the fast phenotype: the variability band/slider (which reads this range) must not drag the parent to a slow-acetylator half-life while fm stays pinned at the fast value 0.40 — that would be an internally inconsistent phenotype state, and the slider varies half-life WITHIN the selected population, not acetylator status. The SLOW acetylators (3.6 ± 1.0 h same study, fm ~0.20) are no longer a prose-only contrast: they are the selectable `slow-acetylator` preset under variability.phenotypes, which swaps this half-life AND fm together so the inconsistent mixed state stays unreachable. These values remain the FAST ones, because the first preset is the default and is defined as the compound's base values. The FDA label quotes a general 3–4 h (not split by phenotype). Consistent with Lima 1979's fast-acetylator total clearance 34.8 L/h and the ~2 L/kg volume (ln2·140 L / 34.8 ≈ 2.8 h). Clearance is NOT stored (t½ gives ke; storing CL too would be a circular cross-check) — the fast-acetylator CL is recorded here for transparency.

vd.conditions: Apparent volume of distribution 'approximately 2 liters per kilogram body weight' (FDA procainamide-HCl label). Not affected by acetylator phenotype (Lima 1979). Scaled to absolute litres against the 70 kg reference subject at derivation time (≈140 L). Procainamide's IV kinetics are genuinely two-compartment (a rapid distribution phase, ~5 min per the label); the oral route absorbs slowly enough to mask that phase, so a one-compartment model fits the oral profile (see notes — the same documented monophasic collapse as cefotaxime/ibuprofen), and ~2 L/kg is the volume that reproduces the observed oral concentrations.

F.conditions: Oral bioavailability ~83% for immediate- and sustained-release procainamide (Bauer, Applied Clinical Pharmacokinetics; range 75–95%). Procainamide is nearly completely ABSORBED; the <100% F is incomplete absorption, NOT first-pass acetylation (hepatic acetylation acts on circulating drug, and first-pass conversion to NAPA is minimal) — so there is NO pre-systemic (firstPassFraction) term: NAPA forms systemically via fm·CL·C_p only. Drives the oral parent curve (C ~ F·Dose/Vd) and the systemic NAPA formation.

tmax.conditions: Peak plasma procainamide at 90–120 minutes after oral immediate-release tablets/capsules (FDA label); 1.5 h used, from which ka is derived. Absorption is slower than procainamide's intrinsic distribution phase, which is why the oral profile reads as one-compartment (notes).

presets.label: Fast acetylator
presets.description: The rapid-NAT2 population: procainamide is acetylated to NAPA about twice as fast, so the parent clears quicker (half-life ~2.4 h) and much more NAPA forms — enough that the NAPA line climbs above the parent. The striking half of the contrast.

presets.label: Slow acetylator
presets.description: The slow-NAT2 population: acetylation runs at roughly half the rate, so procainamide lingers (half-life ~3.6 h, higher parent exposure) while only about half as much NAPA is formed. Parent up, metabolite down — from one inherited enzyme difference.

halfLife.conditions: Procainamide elimination half-life 3.6 ± 1.0 h in SLOW acetylators after a single 750 mg oral dose in healthy subjects (Wierzchowiecki 1980) — the same study and design as the fast-acetylator 2.4 ± 0.7 h this preset contrasts with, which is why the pair is comparable at all. The range [2.6, 4.6] is that 3.6 ± 1.0 h SLOW-acetylator variability, and — exactly as for the fast preset — it is deliberately kept WITHIN the slow phenotype: the variability slider varies half-life within the SELECTED population, never across populations (crossing that boundary is what the preset does, and it swaps fm at the same time). Bauer quotes a longer slow-acetylator half-life (~5.2 h), outside this study's spread and not used, to keep both presets on one source. Consistent with Lima 1979's slow-acetylator clearance 22.6 L/h: CL_slow/CL_fast = 22.6/34.8 = 0.65 agrees with the half-life-implied 2.4/3.6 = 0.67 to within 3%, an independent check that the two presets are one coherent model rather than two studies stitched together. Clearance is NOT stored (t½ gives ke; storing CL too would be circular — and derivation prefers a stored CL over half-life, which would silently discard this override).

param.conditions: In SLOW acetylators the fraction of procainamide clearance proceeding by acetylation to NAPA is 0.20 (Lima 1979 — a formation-CLEARANCE fraction, the correct fm, and the same measure as the fast-acetylator 0.40 it halves). MOLAR, so MW-adjusted to the engine's mass basis on the file's convention: 0.20 × 277.37/235.33 = 0.20 × 1.179 = 0.236 = 23.6% mass (derived: true). NO RANGE is stored, deliberately: the fast preset's range low end is anchored on the FDA label's fast-acetylator urinary-recovery floor, and the slow-acetylator recovery figures (FDA label 16–21% of dose; Wierzchowiecki 24 h urinary NAPA 8.8 ± 5.4%) are lower bounds on a DIFFERENT basis that cannot be converted into a formation-fraction range without inventing one — so the point value ships alone rather than carry a fabricated spread. fm has no slider in any case; the range is provenance, not interaction.

variability.notes: NAT2 acetylator status is procainamide's dominant pharmacogenetic variable and the reason it is in this project. It is modelled as two illustrative POPULATION archetypes (fast/slow), not as a patient-level genotype: choosing a preset is the same kind of educational population choice as the 70 kg reference subject. Vd is NOT overridden by either preset — Lima 1979 found the volume of distribution unaffected by acetylator phenotype — and NAPA's own disposition is likewise phenotype-independent (it is cleared renally, not acetylated), so both presets share them. That is why only half-life and fm move.

metabolites.name: N-acetylprocainamide (NAPA)
metabolites.description: The active metabolite of procainamide, formed by acetylation in the liver — itself a Class III antiarrhythmic (it prolongs repolarisation rather than blocking sodium channels). Cleared by the kidneys and longer-lived than procainamide, so it builds up in kidney failure.

fractionFormed.conditions: In FAST acetylators the fraction of procainamide clearance that proceeds by acetylation to NAPA is 0.40 (Lima 1979 — a formation-CLEARANCE fraction, the correct fm; in slow acetylators it is 0.20, roughly half — the pharmacogenetic teaching point). This is a MOLAR fraction, MW-adjusted to the engine's mass basis: NAPA (MW 277.37) is heavier than procainamide (235.33) — acetylation adds an acetyl group — so mass-fm = 0.40 × 277.37/235.33 = 0.40 × 1.179 = 0.472 = 47.2%. derived: true (molar figure MW-adjusted), the morphine/caffeine convention. NOTE the reported URINARY RECOVERY of NAPA (FDA label 24–33% of dose for fast acetylators; Wierzchowiecki 22.5%) is LOWER — it is a lower bound on the fraction formed, because NAPA is further eliminated rather than fully recovered (the cefotaxime desacetyl-recovery distinction); the range low end (33% mass ≈ molar 0.28) reflects that recovery floor, the point value the formation-clearance fraction. This is the FAST-acetylator value, which is the default because the base values ARE the default phenotype; the slow-acetylator value (~half: 23.6% mass) is the `slow-acetylator` preset under variability.phenotypes, applied atomically with that phenotype's half-life. It is not swept by the half-life slider, which varies half-life only, within one population.

vd.conditions: NAPA's OWN steady-state volume of distribution ~1.4 L/kg (Bauer, Applied Clinical Pharmacokinetics; ~1.46 L/kg by a three-compartment fit) — a true metabolite volume, directly measured because NAPA was itself marketed as the antiarrhythmic acecainide and given directly to humans. Smaller than procainamide's ~2 L/kg. Its own one-compartment disposition, not the parent's. Scaled to absolute litres against the 70 kg reference subject (≈98 L).

halfLife.conditions: NAPA elimination half-life 7.0 ± 1.0 h, directly measured after an oral NAPA (acecainide) dose in healthy subjects (Wierzchowiecki 1980; Bauer quotes ~6 h). This is LONGER than procainamide's ~2.4 h, so NAPA is ELIMINATION-rate-limited (its own elimination k_m is slower than the parent formation rate): the metabolite line tracks NAPA's own slower disposition, and the engine's terminal slope = min(k_parent, k_metabolite) = k_metabolite reproduces this (the cefotaxime/desacetyl pattern, opposite to acetaminophen's formation-rate-limited conjugates). NAPA is cleared almost entirely by the kidneys (≈85% excreted unchanged, glomerular filtration + tubular secretion), so its half-life lengthens dramatically in renal impairment (~41 h) — the reason NAPA ACCUMULATES and carries torsades-de-pointes risk in renal failure. The stored value is the normal-renal-function half-life.

metabolites.notes:
N-acetylprocainamide (NAPA, acecainide) is procainamide's ACTIVE metabolite and pharmacologically DISTINCT from it: procainamide is a class Ia sodium-channel blocker, but NAPA is a pure class III agent (it prolongs the action-potential duration / QT without the sodium-channel block). It was itself developed and marketed as the antiarrhythmic acecainide, so its disposition (Vd ~1.4 L/kg, t½ ~7 h, ~85% renal-unchanged) is DIRECTLY MEASURED, not inferred from parent dosing — there is no metabolite-volume identifiability problem here (contrast acetaminophen's conjugates). Drawn as a dashed 'active metabolite' line.

ELIMINATION-RATE-LIMITED. NAPA's half-life (~7 h) exceeds procainamide's (~2.4 h), so — like desacetylcefotaxime and unlike the acetaminophen conjugates — its plasma decline reflects its OWN slower elimination, and it ACCUMULATES relative to the parent. In a fast acetylator NAPA plasma levels come to exceed procainamide's (modelled molar AUC ratio ~1.8×), the classic clinical observation. Because NAPA is renally cleared, in renal impairment its half-life balloons to ~41 h and it accumulates to high, long-lasting, potentially pro-arrhythmic (torsades) concentrations — the honest reason the intrinsic normal-renal half-life is stored (the renal-failure value is a separate condition, not NAPA's baseline disposition).

fm — MASS CONVERSION + PHENOTYPE. The formation fraction is the acetylation clearance fraction (fast 0.40, Lima 1979), MW-adjusted ×1.179 (NAPA heavier) → 47.2% mass. It is acetylator-PHENOTYPE-dependent (slow ~0.20, ~half), which is exactly this compound's teaching value. The engine still takes ONE fm — that has not changed — but the phenotype is now chosen at the data layer: `variability.phenotypes` re-anchors half-life and fm together before derivation, so the engine sees one coherent population's numbers and never learns what a genotype is (see compound notes for the bright-line framing).

fda_label.title: Procainamide Hydrochloride Injection — DailyMed Structured Product Label, clinical pharmacology: apparent volume of distribution approximately 2 L/kg (reached with a ~5 min distribution half-time); oral peak plasma at 90–120 minutes; elimination half-life 3–4 h with normal renal function; 30–60% excreted unchanged as procainamide; percent metabolised to NAPA 24–33% (fast acetylators) and 16–21% (slow acetylators) — urinary recovery.

lima_1979.title: Lima JJ, Conti DR, Goldfarb AL, Tilstone WJ, Golden LH, Jusko WJ. Clinical pharmacokinetics of procainamide infusions in relation to acetylator phenotype. J Pharmacokinet Biopharm. 1979;7(1):69–85 (PMID 458558). Total procainamide clearance 34.8 L/h (fast acetylators) vs 22.6 L/h (slow); fraction of procainamide clearance proceeding by acetylation to NAPA 0.4 (fast) vs 0.2 (slow); volume of distribution not affected by acetylator phenotype.

wierzchowiecki_1980.title: Wierzchowiecki M, Michałowska D, Łowicki Z, Ochotny R, Grześkowiak A, Tomaszkiewicz T. Pharmacokinetic studies of procainamide (PA) and N-acetylprocainamide (NAPA) in healthy subjects. Int J Clin Pharmacol Ther Toxicol. 1980;18(6):272–276 (PMID 6161089). Single oral doses (PA 750 mg, n=10; NAPA 900 mg, n=6): procainamide half-life 2.4 ± 0.7 h (fast acetylators) vs 3.6 ± 1.0 h (slow); 24 h urinary NAPA 22.5 ± 5.8% (fast) vs 8.8 ± 5.4% (slow); NAPA half-life 7.0 ± 1.0 h.

acecainide_review_1990.title: Harron DW, Brogden RN. Acecainide (N-acetylprocainamide). A review of its pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic properties, and therapeutic potential in cardiac arrhythmias. Drugs. 1990;39(5):720–740 (PMID 1693889). N-acetylprocainamide is a Class III antiarrhythmic (active), eliminated primarily by renal excretion.

bauer_clinpk.title: Bauer LA. Applied Clinical Pharmacokinetics — Procainamide / N-acetyl Procainamide chapter (hosted at basicmedicalkey): oral bioavailability ~83% (immediate- and sustained-release); procainamide volume of distribution ~2.7 L/kg (whole body), fast-acetylator half-life ~2.7 h, slow ~5.2 h, linear pharmacokinetics at therapeutic doses; NAPA volume of distribution ~1.4 L/kg, half-life ~6 h (normal renal function), ~41 h in renal failure, eliminated renally.

metabolism: Procainamide is cleared two ways: about half is excreted unchanged by the kidneys, and much of the rest is acetylated in the liver to N-acetylprocainamide (NAPA), an active metabolite drawn here as a dashed line. NAPA is not just a breakdown product — it is a Class III antiarrhythmic in its own right (it prolongs repolarisation, a different mechanism from procainamide's sodium-channel block), and it was even marketed separately as acecainide, which is why its own kinetics are directly known. NAPA outlives procainamide (half-life ~7 h vs ~2.4 h), so it accumulates and, in a fast acetylator, its plasma level comes to exceed the parent's — the model shows the NAPA line rising above procainamide over time. The acetylation rate is set by an inherited enzyme (NAT2): 'fast acetylators' convert roughly twice as much procainamide to NAPA as 'slow acetylators', a classic pharmacogenetic difference. You can switch between the two illustrative acetylator populations in the controls, and the contrast is worth watching closely: the slow acetylator shows MORE procainamide (it is cleared more slowly, half-life ~3.6 h vs ~2.4 h) but LESS NAPA (only about half as much is formed). Parent up, metabolite down, from a single inherited enzyme difference — and the two move in opposite directions, which is why 'a slow metaboliser has more drug in them' is too simple a story once an active metabolite is in the picture. Because NAPA is cleared by the kidneys, it accumulates dramatically in kidney failure (half-life ~41 h) — the main reason procainamide/NAPA can cause the dangerous arrhythmia torsades de pointes in that setting. This is an educational illustration of inherited metabolic variability, not a patient-specific prediction.
notes:
The 40th compound and the classic PHARMACOGENETICS teaching pair: one parent forming one ACTIVE metabolite (NAPA) at a genetically-polymorphic rate. A NEW compound (user-authorised expansion beyond the 'metabolites for existing compounds' task — it was the best pair in the acetylator/CYP2D6 category the user picked, and NAPA's directly-measured disposition makes it the cleanest).

WHY THIS IS NOT THE ACETAMINOPHEN RECIPE (advisor-flagged — don't blur the two). NAPA is the CEFOTAXIME pattern, not the formation-rate-limited APAP pattern: NAPA's half-life (~7 h) is LONGER than procainamide's (~2.4 h), so it is ELIMINATION-rate-limited and its own disposition governs its decline — and because NAPA was marketed as acecainide, that disposition (Vd ~1.4 L/kg, t½ ~7 h) is DIRECTLY MEASURED, so there is NO assumed-volume / identifiability problem (contrast the acetaminophen conjugates, whose Vd had to be assumed). The APAP formation-limited gate (metabolite t½ below parent) does NOT apply here; the only special gate is the polymorphic fm.

MODEL — ONE-COMPARTMENT ORAL (a documented collapse). Procainamide given intravenously is genuinely two-compartment (a rapid ~5 min distribution phase; the reason the old rejection log flagged the pair as '2-compartment'). But given ORALLY the absorption is slower than that distribution phase, which masks it, so the oral profile reads as one-compartment — the same monophasic-collapse posture as cefotaxime/ibuprofen. So the compound is shipped ORAL-ONLY at one-compartment (F 83%, Tmax 1.5 h → ka); the genuinely-biphasic IV route is omitted rather than misrepresented by a one-compartment fit. Vd ~2 L/kg (FDA label) reproduces the oral concentrations.

BOTH ACETYLATOR PHENOTYPES, AS SELECTABLE PRESETS (the bright-line framing). Acetylation is bimodal (NAT2), and the engine still takes a single fm — but the choice of phenotype now lives in the data layer rather than in the curator's head. `variability.phenotypes` carries two illustrative populations, and selecting one re-anchors parent half-life AND fm together, before derivation, so the engine only ever sees one coherent population's numbers and never learns what a genotype is. Each preset's inputs stay mutually consistent, which is the whole point: fast = fm 0.40 + t½ 2.4 h (Lima's fast CL 34.8 L/h agrees), slow = fm 0.20 + t½ 3.6 h (Lima's slow CL 22.6 L/h agrees — 22.6/34.8 = 0.65 vs the half-life-implied 2.4/3.6 = 0.67, within 3%, an independent check that the pair is ONE model and not two studies stitched together). NAPA's own disposition and the parent Vd are phenotype-INDEPENDENT (Lima 1979 found Vd unaffected by acetylator status), so neither preset touches them. The FAST acetylator is the DEFAULT — it gives the striking picture (more NAPA formed, NAPA exceeding the parent) — and it is the default in the strict sense that its preset overrides NOTHING: the base values above ARE the fast phenotype, so the default curve is this compound exactly as it shipped before presets existed.

WHY PRESETS RATHER THAN A WIDER BAND (the catch this closes). The half-life range drives the variability slider. If the range spanned both phenotypes, the slider would drag the parent to a slow half-life while fm stayed pinned at the fast 0.40 — a state no real person occupies. So each preset's range is kept strictly inside its own phenotype, and crossing between phenotypes is the preset's job, which swaps every affected parameter at once. The two controls compose and must read that way on screen: the PRESET picks which population, the SLIDER is the spread within it.

Still an educational population choice — like the 70 kg reference subject — NOT patient genotyping. A preset is 'here is what the fast-acetylator population looks like', never 'here is what YOUR genotype does'; the UI must never ask for or claim to use a user's genotype. The bright line holds (no 'your genotype → your dose').

fm — MASS CONVERSION + THE LOWER-BOUND DISTINCTION. The stored fm is the acetylation CLEARANCE fraction (fast 0.40, Lima 1979), MW-adjusted ×277.37/235.33 = ×1.179 (NAPA heavier than procainamide) → 47.2% mass. The FDA label's '24–33% metabolised to NAPA' and Wierzchowiecki's 22.5% urinary NAPA are LOWER bounds (urinary recovery < fraction formed, because NAPA is further eliminated) — the same recovery-vs-formation distinction as cefotaxime's desacetyl; the fm range low end reflects that floor.

LINEARITY. Procainamide PK is dose-proportional at therapeutic doses (Bauer; FDA), so linear superposition is valid; concentration-dependent plasma-protein binding is minor. It is a narrow-therapeutic-index drug (therapeutic ~4–10 mg/L; NAPA adds its own window and toxicity), monitored clinically.

SALT. Procainamide is dosed clinically as the hydrochloride (MW 271.79); this model works in mg of procainamide base (MW 235.33), as the PK parameters are reported for the base/moiety — a 750 mg procainamide-HCl dose is ≈650 mg base. Doses entered here are procainamide base.

MAGNITUDE CHECK (built engine curve). 750 mg oral (fast acetylator): parent peaks ~2.9 mg/L at ~1.5 h (within the therapeutic 4–10 mg/L range once repeated dosing accumulates), then NAPA rises behind it and — being elimination-rate-limited (t½ 7 h ≫ parent 2.4 h) — peaks later (~6.5 h), lingers, and its molar AUC comes to ~1.7× the parent's (mass AUC ~1.9×): the NAPA-exceeds-procainamide picture reported in fast acetylators. Educational illustration only — not for dosing.
